greeting: The wagon master looks up from a manifest longer than a psalm. "Bright. If you can drive, lift, or shoot, I can use you. If you can do all three I can love you."
deflect: "Some roads I do not talk about with men I have not shared weather with."

entries.text: "Hosea Bright, captain of the westward trains. Twenty two crossings of the mountains and every wagon accounted for." The pause after it is very small and very deep.
"I take freight and families over the spine of this country and bring the wagons back for more. The TOWN eats because the trains run."

entries.deflect: "Braddock's Road is bad grades and worse memory. We run it in daylight and good company. That is all the answer the road deserves."
entries.text: He caps his ink. "You have shared weather with me now, so. Braddock's Road, west of the Narrows, there is a mile I do not run after dark, and I tell the men it is the grade." He looks at his own hands. "It is not the grade. I lost a man out there once, and the ledger says a storm took him, and the ledger is in my handwriting." He opens the manifest again. "That is all tonight."

He spits toward the depot. "Ten years the B&O's been through, and every year my wagons carry less and the coal cars carry more. They say the rails will reach the Ohio and then what's a wagoner for? I give it a generation. Maybe less." He does not sound wrong.

"Three ways west out of this town, friend, and I have cursed all of them. Braddock's Road, the oldest, bad grades and worse ghosts. The National Road, the government's pike, straight and proud and already crumbling where they cheated on the stone. And the canal, which is not a road but thinks it is." He grins without humor. "All of them begin here. Cumberland is where the country decided to go west, and I am the poor fool it hired to carry it."

"The National Road. Mile zero is that iron marker by the west gate. Congress built it to bind the Ohio country to the seaboard, and for a while it was the making of us: taverns every mile, wagons nose to tail, more traffic than the town could feed." He lowers his voice. "Then the railroad came and the pike went quiet, and quiet roads keep things that busy roads scare off. I still run it. I run it in daylight."
